title: Why You Can't Stop Crying During Kundalini Awakening

body:
Why You Can't Stop Crying During Kundalini Awakening

You're curled up in the dark, probably 3 AM, and the tears just won't stop. They're coming from somewhere deep, a place you didn't even know existed. It's not sadness, not exactly. It's more like a dam breaking, a flood of emotion that feels ancient and brand new all at once. You're wondering if you're losing your mind. I've been there. I know that feeling. That's why you're here, right?

This isn't your garden-variety crying. This is something else entirely, and if you're experiencing a kundalini awakening, or even just what feels like a spiritual emergency, this intense emotional release - especially crying - is incredibly common. It's terrifying, it's exhausting, and it feels like it'll never end. But here's the thing: it's not a sign you're broken. It's a sign you're healing. You're purging. You're shifting. And yeah, it's messy as hell.

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. Kundalini awakening, or any deep spiritual process that shakes your foundations, is often brutal. It's not all bliss and light, despite what some New Age gurus might tell you. It's often a dark night of the soul that lasts for months, sometimes years. And the crying? That's your body and your psyche doing some serious house cleaning. It's a necessary part of the process, even when it feels like it's ripping you apart.

So, let's talk about why you can't stop crying, what's really happening, and how you can try to ride this wave without drowning.

The Deep Clean: What's Really Happening

Look, your body and mind are like a hard drive. Over the years, you've accumulated a ton of junk files: traumas, suppressed emotions, limiting beliefs, societal conditioning, ancestral patterns. All that stuff gets stored. It doesn't just disappear. It sits there, often in your subconscious, influencing everything you do, how you feel, how you react. Kundalini, or this intense spiritual energy, is like a super-powered defragmenter and virus scanner all rolled into one. It's going in there and pulling all that buried crap to the surface.

And when that crap comes to the surface, it's gotta go somewhere. It can manifest as physical sensations, intense heat, vibrations, involuntary movements - all that fun stuff. But often, it comes out as emotion. Raw, unfiltered, overwhelming emotion. And crying is one of your body's primary mechanisms for releasing emotional energy. It's a natural detox. It's not a weakness. It's a biological imperative.

Unprocessed Trauma and Suppressed Emotions

You might think you've dealt with your past. You might've gone to therapy, talked it out, journaled. But some stuff is so deep, so primal, it bypasses your conscious mind entirely. It lives in your nervous system. It lives in your cells. When kundalini energy starts moving, it doesn't ask for permission. It just goes. It hits those old wounds, those places where you shut down, where you froze, where you decided it was safer to not feel. And it forces them open.

You might be crying for things you can't even remember. You might be crying for childhood hurts you thought were long gone. You might be crying for collective pain, for the suffering of humanity. It sounds wild, I know. But when your energetic channels open up, you become more sensitive, more porous. You're feeling things on a much deeper level than before. It's like the volume knob on your emotional experience has been cranked up to eleven. Know what I mean?

I've had clients, and hell, I've had myself, just weep uncontrollably for hours, with no clear reason. No specific memory, no trigger. Just pure, unadulterated release. It's like your soul is finally exhaling decades of holding its breath. And that's okay. It's more than okay; it's necessary. If you try to hold it back, you're just bottling it up again, and it'll come back with more force later. Trust me on this. I tried to suppress it early on. Didn't work out so well. The energy just got more agitated, more intense. It's like trying to hold a beach ball underwater. Eventually, it's going to burst to the surface.

Grief for the Old Self

This is a big one that people often miss. When you're going through a deep spiritual awakening, you're not just shedding old trauma; you're shedding an old identity. The person you thought you were? The life you thought you had? The beliefs that defined you? They're all dissolving. And that's incredibly disorienting. It's a death. And with death comes grief.

You're grieving the loss of your old sense of self, even if that self was miserable. There's a strange comfort in the familiar, even if it's uncomfortable. Now, everything's shifting. Your relationships might be changing, your interests, your worldview. You might not recognize yourself in the mirror. You're in a liminal space, between who you were and who you're becoming. And that's terrifying. It's natural to mourn the loss of what was, even if what's coming is ultimately better. This isn't just intellectual grief; it's felt deep in your bones.

You're shedding layers, like a snake shedding its skin. It's an uncomfortable, vulnerable process. And crying is your body's way of processing that deep sense of loss and transformation. It's a rite of passage, not a breakdown. Though it sure as hell feels like one when you're in the thick of it.

What to Do When the Tears Won't Stop

First off, don't try to stop them. Seriously. I know it's hard. You feel exposed, weak, maybe even embarrassed if someone else is around. But trying to dam up this river of emotion is counterproductive. It's like trying to stop a sneeze once it's started - you're just going to make it worse. Let it flow. Give yourself permission to feel whatever comes up, however ugly or uncomfortable it is.

Create a Safe Space

This is crucial. You need a place where you feel safe enough to completely fall apart. For me, it was my bedroom, door locked, lights off. Sometimes it was just a quiet corner of the house. Wherever it is, make it yours. Tell anyone you live with that you need space, that you're going through something intense and need to be undisturbed. If you live alone, even better - no judgment, just you and your raw emotions.

Have tissues. Have water nearby - you'll get dehydrated from all the crying. Maybe a comfort blanket or a soft pillow. Make it as nurturing an environment as possible. This isn't about fixing anything; it's about allowing. You're creating a container for this intense experience. You might find some comfort in books that describe similar journeys, like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) or even The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). Reading about others who've gone through it can make you feel less alone, honestly.

Ground Yourself

When you're in the midst of a huge emotional release, you can feel completely unmoored. Grounding techniques are your best friend. They won't stop the crying, but they can help you feel a little less like you're floating off into space. Here's what I found helpful:

  • Connect with the Earth: If you can, go outside. Put your bare feet on the grass or soil. Feel the cool dampness. If you can't go outside, imagine roots growing from your feet deep into the earth. Visualize yourself connected, anchored.
  • Physical Sensations: Focus on your breath. Feel the rise and fall of your chest. Notice the texture of the clothes on your skin. Hold a piece of ice in your hand. Splash cold water on your face. These simple physical sensations can bring you back to your body, back to the present moment.
  • Eat Something Substantial: Even if you don't feel like it, eat something grounding - root vegetables, a hearty soup, a piece of toast. Avoid sugar and caffeine; they'll just make your nervous system more agitated.
  • Gentle Movement: If you're able, do some slow, gentle stretches. A little bit of walking. Don't push yourself. The idea is to move the energy through your body, not to exhaust yourself.

These aren't cures, you know. They're just tools to help you stay present and connected to your body while the storm rages. You're not trying to stop the storm; you're just trying to stay upright in the wind.

Seeking Support - The Right Kind

This is where it gets tricky. Not everyone understands what you're going through. Your friends and family might mean well, but they might also be terrified by your intense emotions. They might try to "fix" you or tell you to "just snap out of it." That's not helpful. In fact, it can make you feel even more isolated.

You need support from people who get it. Or at least, people who are open-minded enough to listen without judgment. This might mean:

  • A Therapist Who Understands Spiritual Emergency: Not all therapists are equipped for this. You need someone who is familiar with transpersonal psychology, spiritual crises, or kundalini. Someone who won't pathologize your experience but will help you integrate it. Books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can be good resources to understand what kind of support you might need and what to look for.
  • A Spiritual Teacher or Mentor: Someone who has been through an awakening process themselves and can offer guidance. Be discerning here. There are a lot of charlatans out there. Look for someone grounded, compassionate, and not pushing any specific dogma.
  • Support Groups: Online forums or local groups focused on spiritual awakening or spiritual emergency can be incredibly validating. Just knowing you're not alone, that others have felt this exact same bewildering pain, can be a lifesaver.

Don't be afraid to reach out. You don't have to do this alone. This isn't a sign of weakness; it's a sign of wisdom. You're in uncharted territory, and a guide can make all the difference. I'll tell you what, finding someone who genuinely understood what I was going through was probably the most important thing I did. It gave me permission to just be, to feel, without constantly questioning my sanity.

The Aftermath: Riding the Waves

The crying won't last forever. It might come in waves - intense periods followed by calmer stretches. You'll have moments where you feel completely drained, empty, and then moments where you feel a sense of clarity or peace you've never known before. This is the nature of the process. It's not linear. It's not predictable. It's messy, like I said.

After a big crying session, you might feel exhausted, depleted. That's normal. Your body has just done a huge amount of work. Rest. Allow yourself to recover. Don't try to jump back into your usual routine immediately. Treat yourself with the same kindness and compassion you'd show a sick child. Because in a way, you're healing a very old, very deep sickness.

Trust the process. That's probably the hardest advice to take when you're in the thick of it, when every fiber of your being is screaming for it to stop. But truly, this energy, this awakening, it knows what it's doing. It's clearing out what no longer serves you to make space for something new, something more authentic, something more aligned with your true self. The crying is a release valve. It's essential. It's your body's wisdom in action.

So yeah, you can't stop crying right now because you're shedding years, decades, maybe even lifetimes of accumulated emotional baggage. You're grieving the death of your old self. You're opening up to a depth of feeling you never knew was possible. It's intense, it's uncomfortable, and it's absolutely necessary for the deep transformation you're undergoing. Hang in there. You're not breaking; you're breaking open.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
image_alt: Why You Can't Stop Crying During Kundalini Awakening
